skill.name: aws-sso-login
skill.description: Authenticate AWS CLI profiles with AWS Single Sign-On before running commands that require an active SSO session.
skill.summary: Runs AWS SSO login for a selected AWS CLI profile and verifies the session before AWS CLI work.
skill.author: Daisuke897

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified AWS CLI command examples and access to local AWS SSO configuration. These are expected for an AWS SSO login skill, but they still create operational risk because the skill initiates cloud authentication and relies on local AWS configuration. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, hidden persistence, or unrelated command execution evidence was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: AWS CLI Authentication Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to run aws sso login and related AWS CLI verification commands. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it can authenticate real cloud accounts and should only run with an explicit profile selected by the user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands directly invoke AWS CLI authentication and identity checks. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but the operational impact is clear and should be treated as elevated risk.

medium_findings.title: Local AWS Configuration Dependency
medium_findings.description: The skill requires SSO configuration in ~/.aws/config for the selected profile. This is normal AWS CLI behavior, but it touches a sensitive configuration area and depends on local account setup.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file path is explicitly documented as an AWS SSO prerequisite. It is a sensitive location, although the skill does not instruct the agent to read or transmit its contents.

medium_findings.title: External Authentication Flow Requires Network Access
medium_findings.description: The skill requires network access to the AWS SSO authentication endpoint and a browser-based login flow. This is expected for SSO, but users should understand that the workflow contacts AWS identity services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The prerequisites explicitly require a browser and network access to an SSO endpoint. This supports legitimate authentication and does not show evidence of unknown third-party communication.

low_findings.title: Account Identity Verification Command
low_findings.description: The usage example runs aws sts get-caller-identity to verify authentication. This reveals AWS account identity details locally, but it is a standard verification step and no exfiltration target is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is clearly present and has a predictable identity-disclosure effect. Confidence is high that it is benign because it is framed as local verification and no network destination beyond AWS STS is specified.

low_findings.title: Static Cryptography Finding Appears False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert points to the description line, but that line only describes AWS Single Sign-On. No cryptographic algorithm selection or implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced line contains descriptive metadata for SSO authentication. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic usage in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: External AWS CLI Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents shell commands for aws sso login, aws sts get-caller-identity, and aws sts assume-role. These are not malicious by themselves, but they interact with real AWS accounts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly shown in the skill documentation. Their purpose matches the skill description, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive AWS Configuration Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill references ~/.aws/config as the required SSO profile configuration file. This is expected for AWS CLI SSO but belongs to a sensitive cloud configuration directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path is explicitly named. The skill does not instruct reading secrets, but the directory is sensitive and warrants user awareness.

content.value_statement: AWS CLI sessions expire and can block deployment or inspection work. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through AWS SSO login for the correct profile.
actual_capabilities: Prompts for an AWS CLI SSO profile when the profile is not known.
Runs aws sso login with the selected profile.
Supports browser-based AWS SSO authentication flows.
Shows how to verify authentication with AWS STS.
Documents headless login using the AWS CLI no-browser option.
Explains that AWS SSO sessions are cached locally after login.
limitations: Requires AWS CLI v2 with SSO already configured.
Requires a browser or manual URL access for authentication.
Does not create or repair AWS SSO profile configuration.
Does not bypass expired sessions or automate user approval.

use_cases.title: Refresh a Developer AWS Session
use_cases.description: A developer reauthenticates an expired SSO profile before running AWS CLI commands for a project.

use_cases.title: Prepare Deployment Access
use_cases.description: A release engineer signs in to the correct AWS SSO profile before deployment checks or infrastructure commands.

use_cases.title: Enable Cloud Account Inspection
use_cases.description: A cloud administrator verifies the active AWS identity before inspecting resources or assuming a role.

prompt_templates.title: Log In With a Known Profile
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the aws-sso-login skill to authenticate my AWS CLI profile named web-hosting.

prompt_templates.title: Ask for the Profile First
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the aws-sso-login skill. Ask me which AWS CLI profile to use before running any AWS command.

prompt_templates.title: Verify the Active Identity
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the aws-sso-login skill for my project profile, then verify the AWS identity with STS.

prompt_templates.title: Handle a Headless Environment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the aws-sso-login skill in a headless terminal and provide the manual browser URL if needed.

output_examples.output: The assistant starts AWS SSO login for the web-hosting profile, waits for browser approval, and reports when authentication succeeds.

output_examples.input: My SSO session expired before deployment.
output_examples.output: The assistant asks for the correct profile if needed, runs the login flow, and confirms the session is ready for AWS CLI commands.

output_examples.input: I am on a remote server without a browser.
output_examples.output: The assistant uses the headless option, gives the manual authentication URL, and waits for completion before continuing.

best_practices: Confirm the AWS profile name before running authentication commands.
Verify the AWS identity after login when working across multiple accounts.
Use the no-browser flow only when a browser cannot open on the current machine.
anti_patterns: Do not guess an AWS profile when project context is missing.
Do not run deployment or account-changing commands before SSO login succeeds.
Do not share AWS SSO URLs or account identity output in public channels.

faq.question: Does this skill store AWS credentials?
faq.answer: No. It relies on AWS CLI SSO behavior, which caches session data locally after the user completes authentication.

faq.question: Which AWS CLI version is required?
faq.answer: AWS CLI v2 is required because SSO support depends on version 2 or later.

faq.question: What happens if the profile name is unknown?
faq.answer: The skill should ask the user for the AWS CLI profile before running aws sso login.

faq.question: Can this run without a browser?
faq.answer: Yes, but it must use the no-browser flow and the user must open the provided URL manually.

faq.question: Does this configure AWS SSO for a new profile?
faq.answer: No. The profile must already be configured in the local AWS CLI configuration.

faq.question: Is this safe for production accounts?
faq.answer: Use it only with an intentional profile selection and verify the identity before running account-changing commands.
